# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Explain the structure and function of rods and cones in the human retina.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Rods (1.5 Marks):** $\sim 120$ million photoreceptors responsible for **scotopic (low-light) vision**. Highly sensitive ($\sim$1 photon), achromatic, distributed in peripheral retina, with high neural convergence yielding poor spatial acuity but excellent motion detection.
2. **Cones (1.5 Marks):** $\sim 6$–$7$ million photoreceptors responsible for **photopic (bright-light) vision**. Three types — S, M, L (peaks at 420, 534, 564 nm) enable **trichromatic color vision**. Concentrated at the fovea, low convergence provides high spatial acuity ($\sim 1$ arc-minute MAR).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**What is persistence of vision? Why is it critical for VR display design?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 Mark):** Persistence of vision is the perceptual phenomenon where discrete still images presented at a rate exceeding the **Critical Flicker Fusion (CFF)** frequency are perceived as continuous motion.
2. **Mechanism (1 Mark):** Governed by retinal integration time of photoreceptors — cones $\sim 30$ ms, rods $\sim 100$ ms. The Ferry-Porter law quantifies: $\text{CFF} = 12 + 18\log_{10}(L)$ Hz.
3. **VR/AR Relevance (1 Mark):** HMDs must refresh at $\ge \mathbf{90\ \text{Hz}}$ to prevent perceived flicker, user discomfort, and **simulator sickness** — a hard constraint in the KTU 2024 AR/VR hardware specification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**, OR an integrated 14-mark answer. Two choices are given — attempt ANY ONE.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified]`

**Q. (a) Describe the anatomy of the human eye and explain the optical pathway from cornea to visual cortex.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The human eye is a complex optical instrument with the following structural components:

**1. Outer Tunic (1.5 Marks):**
- **Sclera** — tough white outer layer maintaining shape.
- **Cornea** — transparent front surface, provides $\sim 43$ D of refractive power ($2/3$ of total).

**2. Middle Tunic (1.5 Marks):**
- **Iris** — pigmented, controls pupil diameter ($2$–$8$ mm).
- **Ciliary Body** — muscles that alter lens shape (accommodation).
- **Choroid** — vascular layer nourishing the retina.

**3. Inner Tunic (1.5 Marks):**
- **Retina** — contains photoreceptors (rods + cones), bipolar cells, ganglion cells.
- **Fovea Centralis** — highest cone density, region of sharpest vision ($\sim 0.5°$ central).
- **Optic Disc** — blind spot where optic nerve exits.

**4. Refractive Media (1 Mark):**
- **Aqueous Humor** (anterior chamber), **Vitreous Humor** (posterior chamber) — maintain intraocular pressure and transmit light.

**5. Neural Pathway (1.5 Marks):**
$$\text{Photoreceptors} \to \text{Bipolar cells} \to \text{Ganglion cells} \to \text{Optic Nerve} \to \text{Optic Chiasm} \to \text{LGN} \to \text{V1} \to \text{V2, V4, V5} \to \text{Perception}$$

**Q. (b) Explain the mechanism of accommodation and vergence in the human eye. Discuss the Vergence-Accommodation Conflict (VAC) and its significance in VR/AR design.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Accommodation (2 Marks):**
- The process by which the **ciliary muscles** change the curvature of the crystalline lens to focus on objects at varying distances.
- **Near response:** Ciliary muscles contract $\Rightarrow$ lens becomes more convex $\Rightarrow$ focal power increases ($\sim +33$ D).
- **Far response:** Ciliary muscles relax $\Rightarrow$ lens flattens $\Rightarrow$ focal power decreases ($\sim +19$ D).
- **Range:** $A = P_{\text{near}} - P_{\text{far}}$ Dioptres. Decreases with age (presbyopia).

**2. Vergence (2 Marks):**
- The inward/outward rotation of the eyes to fixate on objects at different depths.
- **Convergence** (near objects): medial rectus muscles rotate eyes inward; vergence angle $\alpha = 2\arctan(I/2D_o)$.
- **Divergence** (far objects): eyes rotate outward.
- Coupled with accommodation via the **near triad** (accommodation + vergence + pupil constriction).

**3. Vergence-Accommodation Conflict (VAC) (2 Marks):**
- In natural vision, the focus distance (accommodation) and the convergence distance (vergence) **always match**.
- In current VR HMDs, the display is at a **fixed optical distance** (e.g., $1.5$ m), so the eye must accommodate at this distance. However, the **stereo disparity** in the rendered image can make the eyes converge at a nearer or farther virtual distance.
- This mismatch between vergence and accommodation cues causes **visual fatigue, eye strain, headaches, and reduced stereoscopic depth perception** — collectively a major source of VR discomfort.

**4. VR/AR Design Solutions (1 Mark):**
- **Varifocal displays** (mechanically moving optics).
- **Light-field / Holographic displays** (multi-focal planes).
- **Multifocal displays** (e.g., Facebook/Meta Half-Dome prototypes).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]`

**Q. (a) Explain the phenomenon of stereopsis in human vision. Derive the stereoscopic depth formula and discuss its application in VR stereo rendering.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Stereopsis Defined (1.5 Marks):**
- Stereopsis is the perception of **binocular depth** arising from the slight horizontal displacement between the images projected on the left and right retinas — known as **retinal disparity**.
- Threshold disparity: $\sim 3$–$5$ arc-seconds in central (foveal) vision.
- Functional range: effective up to $\sim 200$ m (beyond this, other depth cues dominate).

**2. Derivation of Stereoscopic Depth Formula (3 Marks):**

Consider an object at depth $Z$ with retinal image positions $x_L$ and $x_R$ (in mm) in left and right eyes. Let $f$ be the focal length of the eye and $I$ the interocular distance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta_L) &= \frac{x_L}{f} \approx \theta_L \quad \text{(small angle)} \\
\tan(\theta_R) &= \frac{x_R}{f} \approx \theta_R \\
\delta &= \theta_L - \theta_R = \frac{x_L - x_R}{f} \quad \text{(disparity in radians)} \\
\text{By similar triangles in 3D space:} \quad \frac{I}{Z} &= \frac{x_L - x_R}{f} \\
Z &= \frac{I \cdot f}{x_L - x_R} \quad \text{(Stereoscopic depth)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Panum's Fusional Area (1 Mark):**
- Region in visual space within which binocular fusion is possible: $\sim \pm 6$ arc-minutes horizontally, $\sim \pm 3$ arc-minutes vertically. Beyond this, diplopia (double vision) occurs.

**4. Application in VR Stereo Rendering (1.5 Marks):**
- VR engines (Unity, Unreal) render **two offset perspective views** separated by the IPD offset.
- A disparity buffer converts depth $Z$ to horizontal pixel offset: $\Delta x = \dfrac{f \cdot I}{Z} \cdot \text{pixel-pitch}^{-1}$.
- This generates the **stereo image pair** delivered to the HMD's left/right displays.

---

**Q. (b) Discuss the human visual system's color perception mechanism and explain how it informs the color gamut specification of modern AR/VR displays.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Trichromatic Theory (2 Marks):**
- Human color vision is mediated by **three types of cone photoreceptors** with peak sensitivities at:
  - **S-cones (Short):** $\lambda_{\max} \approx 420$ nm (blue)
  - **M-cones (Medium):** $\lambda_{\max} \approx 534$ nm (green)
  - **L-cones (Long):** $\lambda_{\max} \approx 564$ nm (red)
- Any perceived color is the **linear combination** of the three cone responses (Young-Helmholtz theory).

**2. Opponent-Process Theory (1 Mark):**
- At the ganglion/LGN level, signals are encoded in **opponent channels**:
  - Red–Green, Blue–Yellow, Black–White (luminance).
- This explains negative afterimages and color blindness (anomalous trichromacy, dichromacy).

**3. CIE Color Space & Gamut (2 Marks):**
- The **CIE 1931 xy chromaticity diagram** represents all perceivable colors as a 2D horseshoe.
- The perceivable gamut is **larger** than any display can reproduce.
- Standard gamuts:
  - **sRGB:** $\sim 35\%$ of CIE gamut (legacy displays).
  - **DCI-P3:** $\sim 53\%$ (cinema).
  - **Rec. 2020:** $\sim 76\%$ (HDR 4K/8K).
  - **Rec. 2100:** with HLG/PQ transfer functions for HDR.

**4. AR/VR Display Color Considerations (2 Marks):**
- **OLED microdisplays** in modern HMDs (e.g., Meta Quest 3, Apple Vision Pro) achieve near-**DCI-P3** gamut.
- Color calibration must account for **binocular color matching** — both eyes must see identical colorimetry to avoid rivalry.
- For AR pass-through, the **additive** nature of OLED overlaid on real-world light requires **wide gamut + high dynamic range** to compete with ambient sunlight.
- Future **quantum-dot displays** and **microLED** are being adopted to push beyond Rec. 2020.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Conflating Flicker Fusion with Frame Rate:** CFF is a *perceptual* threshold that varies with luminance. Don't say "CFF = 60 Hz" universally — use the **Ferry-Porter Law** for accuracy.
> 2. **Confusing Photopic/Scotopic:** Many students swap rod and cone functions. Remember mnemonic: **"Cones need ConLight"** (bright light); **"Rods Rule in the dark."**
> 3. **Forgetting the Inverted Image:** The retinal image is **inverted and reduced**. The brain re-flips it in V1 — common 1-mark trap.
> 4. **Missing Units in Snellen Conversions:** Always convert MAR to arc-minutes correctly. $1° = 60' = 3600''$ — slip-ups here cost full sub-part marks.
> 5. **Skipping the VAC Discussion:** In any VR-related question, the Vergence-Accommodation Conflict is a **mandatory mention** in 2024 scheme answers.
> 6. **No Diagram = 2 Marks Lost:** Every anatomy question **requires a labelled eye diagram**. A neat, arrow-annotated diagram earns 2–3 easy marks.
> 7. **Wrong Wavelengths:** Visible spectrum is **380–780 nm** (not 400–700 nm — that is the photopic luminous efficiency range).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Visible Spectrum:** $380\text{–}780\ \text{nm}$; photopic peak $\sim 555\ \text{nm}$.
- **Eye Focal Length:** $\sim 17\ \text{mm}$; Total Refractive Power: $\sim 60$ D (43 D cornea + 17 D lens).
- **Photoreceptors:** $120$ M rods (peripheral, scotopic, achromatic) + $6$–$7$ M cones (foveal, photopic, trichromatic).
- **Cone Peaks:** S = $420$ nm, M = $534$ nm, L = $564$ nm.
- **MAR (20/20):** $1$ arc-minute = $1/60°$.
- **Snellen 20/20:** $V = 1$, letter subtends $5'$, stroke subtends $1'$.
- **CFF:** $60\text{–}90$ Hz; follows Ferry-Porter law: $\text{CFF} = 12 + 18 \log_{10}(L)$.
- **Stereopsis:** threshold disparity $3\text{–}5''$; range effective up to $\sim 200$ m.
- **Panum's Fusional Area:** $\sim \pm 6'$ horizontal, $\pm 3'$ vertical.
- **IPD:** Mean $63$ mm (range $51\text{–}77$ mm).
- **Accommodation Range:** $4$ D at age 20, $\to 1$ D at age 60 (presbyopia).
- **Vergence-Accommodation Conflict (VAC):** Major unsolved VR display problem.
- **V1 → V2/V4/V5/MT:** Form, color, motion pathways.
- **Fovea:** $\sim 0.5°$ central high-acuity region; exploited in **foveated rendering**.
- **Dynamic Range:** $10^{-6}\text{–}10^{6}\ \text{cd/m}^2$; mandates HDR displays.
- **Color Gamut Goal:** Rec. 2020 (76% CIE) for next-gen AR/VR HMDs.
- **Latency Budget:** Motion-to-photon $\le 20$ ms to prevent cybersickness.
- **Diagrams to Memorize:** (i) Labelled eye cross-section, (ii) Rod vs Cone comparison table, (iii) Visual pathway from retina to V1, (iv) Depth cues taxonomy, (v) Foveal cone distribution curve.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Color Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Color Theory** in the context of Augmented and Virtual Reality is the mathematical and perceptual framework that defines how light of varying wavelengths is generated, modeled, mixed, quantized, and rendered on display devices to produce a perceptually consistent visual experience for the human observer.

In computer graphics and AR/VR, color theory bridges three fundamental domains:

1. **Physics of Light** — The electromagnetic radiation in the visible spectrum (**380 nm to 750 nm**).
2. **Human Visual Perception** — The biological processing of light by the cone cells (L, M, S) in the retina.
3. **Digital Representation** — The numerical encoding of color using color models and color spaces for storage, transmission, and rendering on HMDs (Head-Mounted Displays) and AR glasses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly requires understanding of color models (RGB, CMY, HSV, YIQ), color spaces, and the mathematics governing conversion between them, as these are foundational for texture mapping, lighting models, and shader programming in VR/AR pipelines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an artist mixing paints. You know that **red + blue = purple**, but only when mixed as pigments. Now imagine switching to a TV screen — there, red, green, and blue light combine to form white. This is the heart of color theory: **the rules of color depend on the medium**.

Think of color as a **three-dimensional recipe book**:
- The **ingredients** are the primary color components.
- The **recipe** is the color model.
- The **kitchen** is the color space (which defines which colors can actually be cooked up on a given device).

For AR/VR, consider the lens of an HMD: every pixel is emitting a tiny recipe of red, green, and blue light. The brain interprets the combined emission as the "true" color of the virtual apple floating in your view.

## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> These terms form the vocabulary evaluated in KTU exams. Memorize their precise meanings.

- **Hue**: The pure color attribute (e.g., red, green, blue) — determined by the dominant wavelength.
- **Saturation**: The purity or intensity of the hue; ranges from gray (low) to vivid (high).
- **Value/Brightness (V)**: The lightness or darkness of a color.
- **Luminance (Y)**: The perceived brightness, weighted by the human eye's spectral sensitivity.
- **Chrominance (C)**: The color information separate from luminance (composed of hue + saturation).
- **Gamma**: The nonlinear encoding curve used to map linear light values to display values (**typically γ ≈ 2.2** for sRGB).
- **Gamut**: The complete range of colors a device can reproduce.

## 1.4 Three Pillars of Color in AR/VR

| Pillar | Domain | Key Constant / Range |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Physical Light | Wavelength of visible EM spectrum | **380 nm – 750 nm** |
| Perceptual | Tristimulus values (X, Y, Z) | CIE 1931 Standard Observer |
| Digital | Bit depth per channel | **8-bit (256 levels)** standard, **10/12-bit** for HDR VR |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Light and Color

Visible light is a narrow band of the electromagnetic spectrum. When this light strikes an object, three things can happen:

1. **Absorption** — Wavelengths are absorbed, converting to heat.
2. **Reflection** — Wavelengths bounce off; the reflected combination determines perceived color.
3. **Transmission** — Light passes through (e.g., glass, water).

The **Spectral Power Distribution (SPD)** $\Phi(\lambda)$ is a function that describes the power emitted at each wavelength $\lambda$. The perceived color of a light source is the integral of its SPD weighted by the eye's response functions.

## 2.2 Human Color Perception — The Tristimulus Theory

The human retina contains three types of cone photoreceptors, each sensitive to a different range of wavelengths:

- **L-cones (Long)**: Peak sensitivity near **564 nm** (red).
- **M-cones (Medium)**: Peak sensitivity near **534 nm** (green).
- **S-cones (Short)**: Peak sensitivity near **420 nm** (blue).

Any visible color can be described by the combined stimulation of these three cone types — this is the **Tristimulus Theory of Color Vision** (Young-Helmholtz theory).

The CIE 1931 standard formalized this with three color-matching functions $\bar{x}(\lambda)$, $\bar{y}(\lambda)$, $\bar{z}(\lambda)$, leading to the XYZ color space used universally for color science.

## 2.3 Additive vs. Subtractive Color Mixing

This is a **high-yield KTU topic**.

### 2.3.1 Additive Color Mixing (RGB)
Used in **light-emitting devices** (monitors, HMDs, projectors, VR headsets). Colors are created by **adding** light components together.

- **Red + Green = Yellow**
- **Green + Blue = Cyan**
- **Red + Blue = Magenta**
- **Red + Green + Blue = White**

### 2.3.2 Subtractive Color Mixing (CMY)
Used in **reflective media** (printing, physical objects). Colors are created by **subtracting** wavelengths using pigments/inks.

- **Cyan + Magenta = Blue**
- **Magenta + Yellow = Red**
- **Cyan + Yellow = Green**
- **Cyan + Magenta + Yellow = Black** (in theory, hence CMYK adds black)

## 2.4 Core Color Models

### 2.4.1 RGB Model (Additive, Device-Dependent)
The most fundamental model for displays. Each pixel is a triplet (R, G, B) with each component typically in [0, 1] or [0, 255].

### 2.4.2 CMY / CMYK Model (Subtractive)
Derived from RGB by subtraction:
$$C = 1 - R, \quad M = 1 - G, \quad Y = 1 - B$$

### 2.4.3 HSV Model (Perceptual)
A cylindrical-coordinate representation designed to be intuitive to humans:
- **H** = Hue (angle, **0° to 360°**)
- **S** = Saturation (**0 to 1**)
- **V** = Value/Brightness (**0 to 1**)

### 2.4.4 YIQ Model (Broadcast TV - NTSC)
Separates luminance (Y) from chrominance (I, Q) for compatibility with black-and-white TV.

### 2.4.5 YUV / YCbCr Model (Digital Video)
Luminance-chrominance model optimized for video compression. Used in **virtually every VR/AR video pipeline** (HDMI, DisplayPort, MJPEG, H.264/HEVC).

### 2.4.6 CIE XYZ and CIE xyY
Device-independent color space anchored to human perception. The **CIE 1931 Chromaticity Diagram** is the canonical map of all perceivable colors.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** The vertical pipe symbol has been replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Tristimulus Integration | $X = \int \Phi(\lambda)\,\bar{x}(\lambda)\,d\lambda$ | $\Phi(\lambda)$ = SPD, $\bar{x}$ = matching function |
| RGB to Normalized Range | $r = R / (R+G+B)$ | r, g, b in [0,1] |
| CIE xy Chromaticity | $x = X / (X+Y+Z)$ | $x + y + z = 1$ |
| RGB $\to$ CMY | $C = 1 - R$ | All values in [0,1] |
| RGB $\to$ HSV (Hue) | $H = 60^\circ \cdot ((G-B) / \Delta) \mod 360$ | $\Delta = \max - \min$ of RGB |
| RGB $\to$ HSV (Saturation) | $S = \Delta / \max(R,G,B)$ | when $\max \neq 0$ |
| RGB $\to$ HSV (Value) | $V = \max(R,G,B)$ | in [0,1] |
| RGB $\to$ YIQ (Luma) | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | Standard NTSC weights |
| RGB $\to$ YIQ (I) | $I = 0.596R - 0.275G - 0.321B$ | In-phase chrominance |
| RGB $\to$ YIQ (Q) | $Q = 0.212R - 0.523G + 0.311B$ | Quadrature chrominance |
| RGB $\to$ YCbCr (SDTV) | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | $R,G,B \in [0,255]$ |
| YCbCr (Chroma) | $Cb = 128 + (-0.169R - 0.331G + 0.500B)$ | Offset by 128 for unsigned |
| YCbCr (Chroma) | $Cr = 128 + (0.500R - 0.419G - 0.081B)$ | Offset by 128 for unsigned |
| Gamma Correction | $V_{out} = V_{in}^{\,1/\gamma}$ | $\gamma \approx 2.2$ for sRGB |

## 2.6 Why This Matters in AR/VR Engineering

- **Color consistency** between virtual objects and the real world is the **single biggest perceptual challenge** in AR (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens, Magic Leap).
- **Color space conversion** is performed on every frame in VR pipelines (OpenXR, OpenGL, Vulkan).
- **HDR (High Dynamic Range)** rendering for next-gen VR headsets (e.g., Apple Vision Pro) uses **10-bit or 12-bit per channel** color with **Rec. 2020** gamut.
- **Color blindness simulation** requires conversion to LMS cone space and remapping.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RGB $\to$ HSV Conversion

This is a **guaranteed KTU exam problem** (either derivation or numerical).

**Given:** $R, G, B \in [0, 1]$ (normalized).

**Step 1:** Find the maximum and minimum channel values.

$$\max = \max(R, G, B)$$
$$\min = \min(R, G, B)$$

These represent the brightest and darkest channel contributions.

**Step 2:** Compute the chroma (range) $\Delta$.

$$\Delta = \max - \min$$

This $\Delta$ is the basis for both hue and saturation.

**Step 3:** Compute Value (V). V is simply the maximum component.

$$V = \max$$

This represents the brightness of the color — what a "value" channel does in HSV picking tools.

**Step 4:** Compute Saturation (S).

$$S = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } \max = 0 \\ \dfrac{\Delta}{\max}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

When $\max = 0$, the color is pure black, so saturation is undefined (set to 0). Otherwise, saturation is the ratio of chroma to brightness.

**Step 5:** Compute Hue (H) using the case-based formula.

$$H = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } \Delta = 0 \\ 60^\circ \cdot \dfrac{G - B}{\Delta} \mod 360, & \text{if } \max = R \\ 60^\circ \cdot \left(2 + \dfrac{B - R}{\Delta}\right), & \text{if } \max = G \\ 60^\circ \cdot \left(4 + \dfrac{R - G}{\Delta}\right), & \text{if } \max = B \end{cases}$$

The hue is calculated based on which channel is dominant. The four cases represent: (1) gray (no chroma), (2) red-dominant, (3) green-dominant, (4) blue-dominant.

**Step 6:** Ensure $H \in [0^\circ, 360^\circ)$. If the result is negative, add 360.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This exact algorithm runs in shader code (HLSL/GLSL) inside VR rendering pipelines to allow real-time color manipulation (e.g., brightness adjustment in passthrough AR).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: RGB $\to$ YIQ Conversion

The YIQ model was defined by the **NTSC (National Television System Committee)** to ensure backward compatibility with monochrome TVs.

**Step 1:** Define the transformation matrix.

The YIQ values are a linear transformation of RGB, expressed in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} Y \\ I \\ Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.299 & 0.587 & 0.114 \\ 0.596 & -0.275 & -0.321 \\ 0.212 & -0.523 & 0.311 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Expand the matrix into three scalar equations.

$$Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$$

The Y (luma) component is a weighted sum. The weights (0.299, 0.587, 0.114) reflect the **photopic luminosity function** — humans are most sensitive to green, then red, then blue.

$$I = 0.596R - 0.275G - 0.321B$$

The I (in-phase) channel encodes orange-cyan chrominance along an axis tilted from the horizontal.

$$Q = 0.212R - 0.523G + 0.311B$$

The Q (quadrature) channel encodes magenta-green chrominance perpendicular to I.

**Step 3:** Derive the inverse transform (YIQ $\to$ RGB).

The inverse matrix is found by inverting the $3 \times 3$ transformation matrix:

$$\begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 & 0.956 & 0.621 \\ 1.000 & -0.272 & -0.647 \\ 1.000 & -1.106 & 1.703 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} Y \\ I \\ Q \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Verify with a known color (pure red, R=1, G=0, B=0).

$$Y = 0.299(1) + 0.587(0) + 0.114(0) = 0.299$$

$$I = 0.596(1) = 0.596$$

$$Q = 0.212(1) = 0.212$$

This is consistent with red being a bright color with significant chroma on the orange-cyan axis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: RGB $\to$ YCbCr Conversion (Used in VR Video Pipelines)

**Step 1:** Compute luma Y using the same Rec. 601 weights as the YIQ luma.

$$Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$$

**Step 2:** Compute the blue-difference chroma Cb.

$$Cb = -0.169R - 0.331G + 0.500B + 128$$

The Cb channel represents the difference between the blue component and luma, offset by 128 to keep the value unsigned for 8-bit storage.

**Step 3:** Compute the red-difference chroma Cr.

$$Cr = 0.500R - 0.419G - 0.081B + 128$$

The Cr channel represents the difference between the red component and luma, similarly offset.

**Step 4:** Verify with white (R=G=B=1).

$$Y = 0.299 + 0.587 + 0.114 = 1.000$$

$$Cb = -0.169 - 0.331 + 0.500 + 128 = 128$$

$$Cr = 0.500 - 0.419 - 0.081 + 128 = 128$$

For neutral white, both chroma channels equal 128 (zero chrominance offset), as expected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **+128 offset** for Cb and Cr. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Convert RGB (0.2, 0.4, 0.8) to HSV

**Step 1:** Find max and min.

$$\max = \max(0.2, 0.4, 0.8) = 0.8 = B$$

$$\min = \min(0.2, 0.4, 0.8) = 0.2 = R$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta$.

$$\Delta = 0.8 - 0.2 = 0.6$$

**Step 3:** Compute V.

$$V = 0.8$$

**Step 4:** Compute S.

$$S = \frac{0.6}{0.8} = 0.75$$

**Step 5:** Compute H (case $\max = B$).

$$H = 60 \cdot \left(4 + \frac{R - G}{\Delta}\right) = 60 \cdot \left(4 + \frac{0.2 - 0.4}{0.6}\right)$$

$$H = 60 \cdot \left(4 - 0.3333\right) = 60 \cdot 3.6667 = 220^\circ$$

**Result:** HSV = **(220°, 0.75, 0.8)** — a saturated, medium-bright blue.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Color Model Taxonomy and Flow in an AR/VR Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scene Linear RGB Float] --> B[Gamma Correction sRGB Encoding]
    B --> C[Display Framebuffer 8-bit per Channel]
    C --> D[HMD Lens Distortion Correction]
    D --> E[Per Eye Display Output]
    F[Camera Input AR Passthrough] --> G[RGB to YCbCr Conversion]
    G --> H[Video Encoder H.264 HEVC]
    H --> I[Network or Storage]
    I --> J[Decoder]
    J --> K[YCbCr to RGB Conversion]
    K --> C
    L[User Hue Saturation Adjustment] --> M[RGB to HSV Conversion]
    M --> N[Modify H S V Components]
    N --> O[HSV to RGB Conversion]
    O --> A
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[RGB Input 0 to 255] --> S1[Normalize to 0 to 1]
    S1 --> S2{Choose Target Model}
    S2 --> P1[CMY: C=1-R, M=1-G, Y=1-B]
    S2 --> P2[HSV: Max, Delta, Sector]
    S2 --> P3[YIQ: Matrix Multiply]
    S2 --> P4[YCbCr: Y then offset chroma]
    S2 --> P5[CIE XYZ: Integration of SPD]
    P5 --> P6[CIE xyY: Normalize X+Y+Z]
    P1 --> O1[Output]

    P3 --> O1
    P4 --> O1
    P6 --> O1
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Hue around circumference 0 to 360 degrees]
        A1[0 deg Red]
        A2[60 deg Yellow]
        A3[120 deg Green]

        A6[300 deg Magenta]
    end

```
