## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Augmented Reality and Virtual Reality with any three parameters.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (precise, exam-ready phrasing):**

| Parameter | Augmented Reality (AR) | Virtual Reality (VR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Environment** | Real world with virtual overlays | Fully synthetic, computer-generated |
| **Immersion Level** | Partial — user remains aware of surroundings | Complete — user is isolated from reality |
| **Hardware** | Smart glasses, smartphones, HUDs | Opaque HMDs (Head-Mounted Displays) |
| **Compositing** | $I_{\text{out}} = (1-\alpha)I_{\text{real}} + \alpha I_{\text{virtual}}$, $\alpha < 0.5$ | $I_{\text{out}} = I_{\text{virtual}}$, $\alpha = 1$ |

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. State Milgram's Reality–Virtuality Continuum. What are its four zones?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Reality–Virtuality Continuum, proposed by **Milgram and Kishino (1994)**, is a taxonomic model that classifies visual displays based on the proportion of real-world versus computer-generated imagery in any mixed scene. The four zones, from left (pure real) to right (pure virtual), are:
1. **Real Environment** ($\alpha = 0$)
2. **Augmented Reality (AR)** — real-dominated, virtual added
3. **Augmented Virtuality (AV)** — virtual-dominated, real elements injected
4. **Virtual Environment (VE / VR)** ($\alpha = 1$)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, ESE Pattern, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A: (a)** Explain the Reality–Virtuality Continuum with a neat diagram. Compare and contrast AR, AV, MR, and VR. *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the Continuum.**
The Reality–Virtuality Continuum (Milgram & Kishino, 1994) is a one-dimensional spectrum characterizing any visual display by the degree to which it substitutes or augments the user's view of the real world. It is parameterized by a virtuality coefficient $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 2 — Write the Mixing Equation.**

$$
I_{\text{out}}(u, v, t) = \big(1 - \alpha(u, v, t)\big) \cdot I_{\text{real}}(u, v, t) \;+\; \alpha(u, v, t) \cdot I_{\text{virtual}}(u, v, t)
$$

**Step 3 — Describe Each Zone.**

- **Real Environment ($\alpha = 0$):** No virtual content; user sees the unmediated physical world (e.g., naked-eye view).
- **Augmented Reality ($\alpha \approx 0.1\text{–}0.4$):** Real scene dominates; virtual annotations (labels, 3D models, arrows) are superimposed. Example: Pokémon GO, AR-HUD.
- **Augmented Virtuality ($\alpha \approx 0.6\text{–}0.9$):** Virtual scene dominates; real elements (e.g., live video of user's hands via passthrough cameras) are inserted. Example: VRChat with passthrough.
- **Virtual Environment ($\alpha = 1.0$):** Fully synthetic; user is completely immersed. Example: Half-Life: Alyx, Meta Quest titles.

**Step 4 — Include the Diagram.**
Refer to **Mermaid Diagram 1 (Section 4.1)** — the four zones arranged left-to-right with $\alpha$ increasing.

**Step 5 — Summarize Distinctions.**

| Feature | AR | AV | MR | VR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reality dominance | Yes | No | Balanced | No |
| Hardware | OST / VST glasses | VST HMD | Depth-aware HMD | Opaque HMD |
| Occlusion of real by virtual | Optional | Required | Required | N/A |

---

#### **Question A: (b)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the architecture of an Augmented Reality system. List the major hardware and software components. *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction.**
An AR system continuously performs: **Capture → Track → Align → Render → Composite → Display**. It must do so at ≤ **20 ms** motion-to-photon latency to avoid misalignment artefacts.

**Step 2 — Draw / Describe the Block Diagram.**
Refer to **Mermaid Diagram 2 (Section 4.2)** for the layered pipeline:
- **Sensor Layer:** RGB camera, depth sensor (LiDAR / ToF / Structured Light), IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope), magnetometer.
- **Fusion & Pose Layer:** Kalman/Complementary filter for IMU smoothing; SLAM module for environment mapping and 6-DoF camera pose estimation.
- **Rendering & Compositing Layer:** 3D scene renderer that produces virtual content from the user's pose; registration module that aligns virtual content with real-feature coordinates; compositor that blends per-pixel using $\alpha$.
- **Output Layer:** Optical See-Through (OST) HMD, Video See-Through (VST) HMD, or smartphone/tablet screen.

**Step 3 — Hardware Components.**

| Component | Typical Specification |
|---|---|
| RGB Camera | ≥ **1080p @ 60 Hz**, global shutter preferred |
| Depth Sensor | LiDAR / Time-of-Flight / Structured Light |
| IMU | 6-DoF or 9-DoF, ≤ **1000 Hz** sampling |
| Processor | Mobile SoC (Snapdragon XR2, Apple M-series) or PC-tethered |
| Display | Micro-OLED / LCoS / Diffractive Waveguide |
| Optics | Birdbath, pancake, or holographic waveguide |

**Step 4 — Software Components.**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| SLAM Engine (ORB-SLAM3, ARKit, ARCore) | Real-time camera tracking and mapping |
| Computer-Vision Pipeline | Feature detection, descriptor matching |
| 3D Renderer (Unity, Unreal) | Virtual content rendering |
| Compositor | Pixel-level blending with depth occlusion |
| Application Layer | Domain logic (medical, industrial, gaming) |

**Step 5 — Conclude.**
The success of an AR system is governed by the tight integration of accurate **tracking**, low-latency **rendering**, and pixel-accurate **compositing** — each subsystem must respect the **20 ms total latency budget**.

---

#### **Question B: (a)** Explain the operational pipeline of a Virtual Reality system. How does it achieve the sensation of "presence"? Discuss the role of head tracking and stereoscopic rendering. *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Presence.**
Presence is the **psychological state** in which a user, despite being physically located in one environment, *behaves and feels* as if they are located inside a different (virtual) environment. It is the **defining goal** of VR.

**Step 2 — State the Four-Stage VR Pipeline.**
1. **Pose Capture:** 6-DoF HMD pose $(p, q)$ sampled from IMU + external trackers.
2. **Scene Rendering:** Two perspective views (left, right) generated from $p$ using IPD-offset stereo cameras.
3. **Sensor Fusion:** Complementary or Kalman filter smooths pose to eliminate jitter.
4. **Display:** Synchronized left/right frames presented on dual displays ≤ **11 ms** after motion.

**Step 3 — Role of Head Tracking.**
- **Low latency** (< 20 ms) prevents the disconnect between vestibular and visual cues → prevents simulator sickness.
- **High accuracy** (< 0.1° rotational, < 1 mm positional) ensures virtual objects appear stable when the head moves.
- **Predictive tracking** extrapolates pose during the GPU render to further reduce perceived latency.

**Step 4 — Role of Stereoscopic Rendering.**
- Two slightly offset virtual cameras, separated by $B = $ IPD ($\approx$ **64 mm**), simulate human binocular disparity.
- The brain fuses the two images via the **stereopsis** mechanism, perceiving depth.
- Depth is governed by $Z = \dfrac{B \cdot f}{d_{\text{disp}}}$ — the smaller the disparity, the farther the object.

**Step 5 — Achieved Presence.**
Presence emerges when:
- **Visual cues** (stereo, FoV ≥ 110°, high PPD ≥ 20) match **vestibular cues** (low-latency tracking).
- **Audio** is spatialized via HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function).
- **Haptics** optionally confirm physical interactions.

**Step 6 — Conclude.**
VR achieves presence by *replacing* — not *augmenting* — reality through synchronized multi-sensory substitution with sub-perceptual latency.

---

#### **Question B: (b)** Compare and contrast AR and VR across **eight** distinct technical parameters. Which is more suitable for surgical navigation, and why? Justify with quantitative reasoning. *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the Eight Parameters.**

| # | Parameter | AR | VR |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reality-Virtuality $\alpha$ | 0.05 – 0.4 | 1.0 |
| 2 | Display Type | Optical / Video See-Through | Opaque HMD |
| 3 | Immersion | Partial | Complete |
| 4 | Mobility | High (untethered) | Constrained (room-scale) |
| 5 | Tracking | 6-DoF + environment map | 6-DoF head-only |
| 6 | Latency Budget | ≤ 20 ms | ≤ 11 ms |
| 7 | Registration Error Tolerance | ≤ 1 mm at 1 m | ≤ 5 mm (no real reference) |
| 8 | GPU Load | Moderate (overlay) | Extreme (full scene) |

**Step 2 — Surgical Navigation Requirements.**
A surgeon must simultaneously:
- See the **patient's body** (real tissue, blood, instruments).
- See **pre-operative CT/MRI data** registered to the patient's anatomy (e.g., tumor boundaries, vessel paths).
- Maintain **sterility** and use **both hands**.

**Step 3 — Why AR is Superior — Quantitative Justification.**
Using the registration error formula $E_{\text{reg}} = \dfrac{Z \cdot \Delta u}{f_x}$:

For surgical AR at $Z = 0.3$ m (working distance), with a typical endoscope focal length $f_x = 1000$ px, even a 1-pixel misalignment yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{reg}} &= \frac{0.3 \cdot 1}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0003 \text{ m} = 0.3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This sub-millimetre error is acceptable for surgical-grade AR — and *necessary*, because the surgeon needs real-world tissue as the ground truth. VR would **eliminate** the view of the patient, making physical surgery impossible.

**Step 4 — Use Cases Where VR Wins.**
VR is preferable for **pre-operative rehearsal** (surgeon explores a 3D reconstruction of the patient's anatomy) and **skills training** (laparoscopic simulator), where the real patient is *not* present.

**Step 5 — Conclude.**
**AR is the correct choice for live surgical navigation** because it preserves the real-world ground truth, provides sub-millimetre registration with current hardware, and aligns with the surgeon's need for simultaneous real-and-virtual visual channels. VR is reserved for rehearsal and training.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on "AR vs VR" Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating AR with MR.** AR augments reality; MR anchors virtual objects *into* reality with occlusion. Many answers treat them as identical → loses 2-3 marks per question.
> 2. **Omitting the Continuum diagram.** When asked to "explain with a neat diagram," students write only text. KTU examiners *mandate* a labeled diagram of the Milgram continuum — non-negotiable 2-mark component.
> 3. **Confusing latency budgets.** AR = **20 ms** (tracking-to-overlay); VR = **11 ms** (motion-to-photon). Mixing these up reveals a shallow understanding.
> 4. **Forgetting quantitative reasoning.** When asked "which is better for X," a paragraph of qualitative text without the registration-error formula or a numeric example loses the application-mark (typically 2-3 of 7).
> 5. **Mixing up FoV ranges.** VR HMDs target **110° – 210°** (match human peripheral vision); optical see-through AR typically delivers only **40° – 60°** FoV. Reversing these values is a classic examiner trap.
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" behind VR = presence.** Simply stating "VR is immersive" is worth **0 marks**. You must explain the multi-sensory substitution + sub-perceptual latency mechanism.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Milgram's Reality–Virtuality Continuum** has four zones: **Real → AR → AV → VR**, parameterized by $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.
- [ ] **VR replaces** reality; **AR augments** reality; **MR anchors and occludes** virtual objects in reality.
- [ ] **VR latency budget** ≤ **11 ms** (motion-to-photon); **AR latency budget** ≤ **20 ms** (tracking-to-overlay).
- [ ] **VR refresh rate** ≥ **90 Hz**; **VR FoV** ≥ **110°**; **AR OST FoV** typically **40° – 60°**.
- [ ] **IPD** standard = **64 mm**; comfortable range **51 – 77 mm**.
- [ ] **Pixel Registration Error** = $E_{\text{reg}} = \dfrac{Z \cdot \Delta u}{f_x}$ — this scales linearly with depth.
- [ ] **Stereoscopic Depth** = $Z = \dfrac{B \cdot f}{d_{\text{disp}}}$ — greater disparity ⇒ closer object.
- [ ] **Compositing equation:** $I_{\text{out}} = (1 - \alpha) I_{\text{real}} + \alpha I_{\text{virtual}}$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- [ ] **PPD ≥ 20** for retinal-limit resolution (key for VR text readability).
- [ ] **Simulator sickness** arises when visual-vestibular mismatch exceeds ~**20 ms**.
- [ ] **SLAM** is the foundational algorithm for AR tracking; **forward kinematics + stereo rendering** is the VR baseline.
- [ ] Representative 2024 devices: **Meta Quest 3** (VR/MR), **Apple Vision Pro** (AR/MR/VR), **Microsoft HoloLens 2** (AR/MR), **Magic Leap 2** (AR/MR).
- [ ] Always pair AR/VR/MR answers with a **diagram** and at least **one numerical example** to score full marks under KTU 2024 marking scheme.
- [ ] Remember the **eight-parameter comparison matrix** in §3.4 — it is the single most likely sub-question structure in the 14-mark ESE paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AR Systems and Functionality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AR Systems and Functionality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of AR Systems

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (PECPT634 / Module 3)**
> An **Augmented Reality (AR) System** is a real-time, context-sensitive integration of computer-generated virtual content (2D or 3D) with the user's view of the physical world, executed through a multi-stage sensing–processing–rendering pipeline that preserves spatial registration, temporal coherence, and user-centric interactivity in the **real-world coordinate frame**.

The **Functionality** of an AR system describes the complete set of hardware, software, and algorithmic operations that transform raw sensor input into a perceptually seamless blend of real and virtual imagery. In the **2024 Scheme** KTU syllabus, this is examined under the Course Outcome **CO3**: *"Analyze the architecture and functional pipeline of an Augmented Reality system."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Functional Pillars of an AR System (Azuma's 1997 criteria, extended for KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Combines Real and Virtual** (not purely synthetic as in VR)
> 2. **Interactive in Real-Time** (< **20 ms** motion-to-photon latency)
> 3. **Registered in 3D** (6-DoF pose alignment between virtual and real)
> 4. **Context-Aware** (uses sensors, GPS, depth, semantics)
> 5. **Mobile / Wearable / Stationary Deployable**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Dashboard Head-Up Display (HUD)"

Imagine driving a modern car. A small projector beams your **speed, navigation arrows, and warning signs** onto the windshield. The road is *real*, the numbers are *virtual*, they are *perfectly aligned* with where you are looking, and they *update instantly* with your movement. **That is Augmented Reality.**

Now, the **AR system** is the *entire car computer behind that HUD*:
- The **camera/radar** is the "eye" (input)
- The **processor** is the "brain" (analysis)
- The **HUD/projector** is the "mouth" (output)

In academic terms, an AR System is a **closed-loop cyber-physical pipeline** that continuously samples the world, decides what to augment, and renders the augmentation into the user's view — all within the perceptual threshold of the human eye (≈ **60 Hz**, **< 20 ms** latency, **≥ 60°** FOV for comfortable AR).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize (Module 3 High-Yield):**
> - **Tracking latency:** ≤ **20 ms** (to avoid motion sickness and registration drift)
> - **Refresh rate:** ≥ **60 Hz** (modern AR ≥ 90 Hz)
> - **Pose accuracy:** ≤ **1 mm** translational, ≤ **0.5°** rotational (medical/industrial)
> - **Field of View:** **20°–70°** (current commercial range)
> - **Display brightness:** ≥ **2000 nits** (outdoor AR readability)
> - **Depth cue resolution:** ≤ **2 arc-minutes** (human stereopsis limit)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an AR System — Layered View

The 2024 KTU syllabus treats AR functionality as a **five-layer pipeline**. Each layer is a sub-system that performs a specific transformation on data flowing from sensors to the user's retina.

### Layer 1 — **Sensing / Input Layer** (Data Acquisition)
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis magnetometer → 9-DoF orientation.
- **Monocular / Stereo / RGB-D Camera:** Captures the visual scene.
- **LiDAR / Time-of-Flight (ToF):** Provides depth maps.
- **GPS, Wi-Fi, BLE:** Provides outdoor/global localization.
- **Eye-tracker, Microphone, Bio-sensors:** Provide user state.

### Layer 2 — **Tracking & Registration Layer** (Pose Estimation)
This is the mathematical heart of any AR system. It estimates the **6-DoF pose** $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ of the camera/device relative to a known reference frame.

Common algorithms:
- **Marker-based:** ARToolKit, ArUco (uses fiducials).
- **Markerless / Natural Feature Tracking (NFT):** SIFT, SURF, ORB + RANSAC.
- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping):** ORB-SLAM, LSD-SLAM, Microsoft HoloLens SLAM.
- **Model-based:** Edge tracking of known 3D CAD models.
- **Sensor-fusion:** Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) fusing IMU + Vision.

### Layer 3 — **Scene Understanding / Reasoning Layer**
- **Plane detection** (horizontal, vertical).
- **Object detection / classification** (YOLO, SSD, semantic segmentation).
- **Occlusion handling** (depth-buffer comparison).
- **Lighting estimation** (spherical harmonics, environment maps).

### Layer 4 — **Rendering / Compositing Layer**
- **Virtual content generation** (OpenGL ES, Vulkan, DirectX).
- **Real–virtual blending** (alpha compositing, depth compositing).
- **Occlusion** via depth buffer.
- **Shading / lighting consistency** with real environment.

### Layer 5 — **Display / Output Layer**
- **Optical See-Through (OST):** Optical combiners, waveguides, birdbath.
- **Video See-Through (VST):** Camera pass-through HMD (e.g., Meta Quest passthrough, Varjo XR-3).
- **Handheld / Spatially-Enabled Devices:** Phones, tablets with ARKit/ARCore.
- **Projection-based AR / Spatial AR.**

## 2.2 The Complete AR Functional Pipeline (Data Flow)

$$
\text{Real World} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Sensors}}\; \text{Raw Data} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Tracking}}\; \text{6-DoF Pose} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Scene Understanding}}\; \text{Semantic Map}
$$

$$
\text{Semantic Map} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Rendering}}\; \text{Virtual Content} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Compositing}}\; \text{Augmented Frame} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Display}}\; \text{User's Retina}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for AR Systems & Functionality**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Camera FOV (horizontal) | $\text{FOV}_h = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right)$ | $w$ = sensor width (mm), $f$ = focal length (mm) | Degrees, fundamental optics |
| 2 | Camera FOV (vertical) | $\text{FOV}_v = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$ | $h$ = sensor height (mm) | Used for display sizing |
| 3 | Pinhole projection (image coords) | $u = f\dfrac{X}{Z},\; v = f\dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $(X,Y,Z)$ in camera frame | Foundation of AR rendering |
| 4 | Stereo disparity → depth | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $B$ = baseline, $d$ = disparity (pixels) | Core of stereo AR tracking |
| 5 | Motion-to-photon latency budget | $L_{total} = L_{sensor}+L_{track}+L_{render}+L_{display}$ | All in ms | KTU requires $< 20$ ms |
| 6 | Display refresh period | $T_{frame}=\dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}}$ | $f_{refresh}$ in Hz | At **90 Hz**, $T=11.11$ ms |
| 7 | Pose transformation chain | $\mathbf{T}_{world}^{cam}=\mathbf{T}_{IMU}^{cam}\cdot\mathbf{T}_{world}^{IMU}$ | $4\times 4$ homogeneous matrices | 6-DoF registration |
| 8 | Tracking drift (visual-inertial) | $\sigma_{drift}(t)\approx k\cdot t^{\alpha},\; \alpha\in[1,1.5]$ | $k$ = drift constant | Bounded by loop closure |
| 9 | Virtual image distance (OST) | $\dfrac{1}{d_o}+\dfrac{1}{d_i}=\dfrac{1}{f}$ | $d_o$ = object, $d_i$ = image, $f$ = lens | Thin-lens combiner optics |
| 10 | Vergence-accommodation conflict (VAC) | $\Delta_{\text{VAC}} = \vert d_{vergence} - d_{accommodation} \vert$ | In dioptres (D) or m | Should be $< 0.5$ D |
| 11 | Pixel angular resolution | $\theta_{px}=\dfrac{\text{FOV}}{\text{Resolution}_{px}}$ | Degrees per pixel | "Pixel pitch" in arc-min |
| 12 | Sunlight-readable brightness | $L_{outdoor} \geq 2000\;\text{nits}$ | $1\,\text{nit}=1\,\text{cd/m}^2$ | KTU outdoor AR benchmark |
| 13 | Inertial sensor bias drift | $\omega_{bias}(t)=\omega_{bias}(0)+\beta t$ | $\beta$ = bias stability (°/s²) | EKF compensation needed |
| 14 | Triangulation accuracy (baseline $B$, range $Z$) | $\sigma_Z = \dfrac{Z^2}{f\cdot B}\sigma_d$ | $\sigma_d$ = disparity error | Inverse depth scaling |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| AR Subsystem | Engineering Application | Why It Matters |
|--------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **6-DoF Tracking (SLAM)** | Robotics, autonomous vehicles | Localize without GPS |
| **VST Passthrough** | Industrial training (Siemens, Boeing) | Safe, cheap hazard simulation |
| **OST Waveguide** | Surgical AR (Medivis, AccuVein) | Hands-free, sterile field |
| **Depth Compositing** | AR occlusion in retail (IKEA Place) | Realistic object placement |
| **Sensor Fusion (EKF)** | Drone AR overlays (DJI Goggles) | Stable in vibration |
| **Light Estimation** | VFX pre-visualization | Seamless virtual lighting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway (KTU 2024):** Every functional layer of an AR system must be **optimized jointly**, not in isolation. A brilliant tracker is useless if the display adds 15 ms of lag. KTU board problems therefore test **end-to-end latency budget** and **pipeline bottlenecks**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the most frequently examined equations in AR systems and functionality, with **every intermediate step** explicitly shown, as required by the KTU 2024 evaluation rubric.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View from Focal Length and Sensor Size

### Problem Statement
A smartphone-based AR camera has a focal length $f = 4.2\,\text{mm}$ and a sensor of width $w = 5.25\,\text{mm}$ and height $h = 2.94\,\text{mm}$. Compute the horizontal and vertical field of view.

### Step 1 — State the geometric principle
Light from the optical center forms a cone whose half-angle equals the angle subtended by half the sensor width at the focal plane.

$$
\theta_{h/2} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{w/2}{f}\right)
$$

**Logic:** By similar triangles, the opposite side is half the sensor width, and the adjacent side is the focal length.

### Step 2 — Full horizontal FOV is twice the half-angle

$$
\text{FOV}_h = 2\theta_{h/2} = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{2f}\right)
$$

**Logic:** The full cone of view spans from one edge of the sensor to the opposite edge.

### Step 3 — Substitute the numeric values

$$
\text{FOV}_h = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{5.25}{2 \times 4.2}\right) = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{5.25}{8.4}\right) = 2\arctan(0.625)
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the arctangent

$$
\arctan(0.625) \approx 32.005^\circ
$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by 2

$$
\text{FOV}_h \approx 2 \times 32.005^\circ = 64.01^\circ
$$

### Step 6 — Repeat for vertical FOV

$$
\text{FOV}_v = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{h}{2f}\right) = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{2.94}{8.4}\right) = 2\arctan(0.35) \approx 2 \times 19.29^\circ = 38.58^\circ
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{FOV}_h \approx 64^\circ, \qquad \text{FOV}_v \approx 38.6^\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the published FOV of typical AR smartphones (e.g., iPhone 15 Pro main camera: **~65° horizontal**). The result validates the pinhole model for AR functionality.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End AR Latency Budget

### Problem Statement
An AR system has the following components: sensor read = **3 ms**, tracking = **4 ms**, scene understanding = **2 ms**, rendering = **5 ms**, display update = **2 ms**. Determine whether the system meets the KTU acceptance criterion of **< 20 ms** motion-to-photon latency, and if not, identify the optimization.

### Step 1 — State the additive latency equation

$$
L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{track} + L_{understand} + L_{render} + L_{display}
$$

**Logic:** Each pipeline stage runs sequentially on a single frame (no parallelism at frame level), so latencies add.

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
L_{total} = 3 + 4 + 2 + 5 + 2 = 16\;\text{ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare to the KTU threshold

$$
L_{total} = 16\;\text{ms} < 20\;\text{ms} \;\;\checkmark \;\;\text{Acceptable}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the safety margin

$$
\Delta L = 20 - 16 = 4\;\text{ms}\;\;\text{of headroom}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{L_{total}=16\;\text{ms},\;\;\text{system is KTU-compliant with}\; 4\;\text{ms headroom.}}
$$

### Step 5 — Optimization scenario (typical KTU sub-question)
If the scene-understanding module is upgraded to a deep neural net and latency rises from 2 ms → 6 ms, recompute:

$$
L_{total}^{\text{new}} = 3 + 4 + 6 + 5 + 2 = 20\;\text{ms} \;\;\text{(right at the edge)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Reduction strategies:}\;\; L_{render}\to 4\;\text{ms}\;\;\text{by foveated rendering, OR}\; L_{track}\to 3\;\text{ms}\;\;\text{via hardware acceleration.}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stereo Depth from Disparity (AR Occlusion)

### Problem Statement
An OST AR headset has two cameras with focal length $f = 1100$ pixels, baseline $B = 64\;\text{mm}$. A real object is observed with disparity $d = 8$ pixels. Compute the depth $Z$.

### Step 1 — State the stereo geometry

$$
\frac{d}{f} = \frac{B}{Z}
$$

**Logic:** Similar triangles between the two image planes and the world point.

### Step 2 — Solve for $Z$

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to consistent units (use mm)

$$
Z = \frac{1100 \times 64}{8} = \frac{70400}{8} = 8800\;\text{mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Express in meters

$$
Z = 8.8\;\text{m}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{Z = 8.8\;\text{m}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This depth value is then inserted into the **z-buffer** of the virtual rendering pass so that a virtual object placed behind the real one is correctly **occluded**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Depth Uncertainty Propagation (Tracking Error)

### Problem Statement
If the disparity measurement has uncertainty $\sigma_d = 0.5$ pixel, compute the depth uncertainty $\sigma_Z$ at $Z = 8.8\;\text{m}$, $f=1100$ px, $B=64$ mm.

### Step 1 — Re-state the depth equation

$$
Z = \frac{fB}{d}
$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $d$ (treat $f$, $B$ as constants)

$$
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} = -\frac{fB}{d^2} = -\frac{Z}{d}
$$

**Logic:** The negative sign indicates that increasing disparity reduces depth (object is closer).

### Step 3 — Apply first-order error propagation

$$
\sigma_Z \approx \left\vert \frac{\partial Z}{\partial d}\right\vert \sigma_d = \frac{Z}{d}\sigma_d
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numbers

$$
\sigma_Z = \frac{8.8\;\text{m}}{8} \times 0.5 = 1.1 \times 0.5 = 0.55\;\text{m}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_Z = 0.55\;\text{m at 8.8 m range}}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the result
- At $Z = 8.8$ m, a half-pixel disparity error gives **0.55 m depth uncertainty** — unacceptable for precision AR (medical, assembly).
- **Mitigation:** Wider baseline $B$, longer focal length $f$, or sub-pixel disparity estimation.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Pixel Angular Resolution (Display Sharpness)

### Problem Statement
A see-through AR display has horizontal FOV = **52°** and horizontal pixel count = **2560**. Compute the angular resolution per pixel and compare to the **human eye's** limit (≈ 1 arc-minute = 1/60°).

### Step 1 — Angular per-pixel equation

$$
\theta_{px} = \frac{\text{FOV}_h}{N_{px}}
$$

**Logic:** Distribute the total FOV uniformly across the pixel count.

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
\theta_{px} = \frac{52^\circ}{2560} = 0.02031^\circ/\text{pixel}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to arc-minutes

$$
\theta_{px} = 0.02031^\circ \times 60\;\text{arcmin/deg} = 1.219\;\text{arcmin/pixel}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare to retinal limit

$$
\theta_{px} \approx 1.22\;\text{arcmin} > 1\;\text{arcmin}
$$

**Conclusion:** The display is **just above the diffraction/retinal limit** — pixels are barely resolvable. A "retina-grade" AR display requires $\theta_{px} \leq 1$ arc-min, which would demand $\geq 3120$ pixels across 52° FOV.

$$
\boxed{N_{px,\;\min}=\frac{52^\circ}{1\;\text{arcmin}}\times 60 = 3120\;\text{pixels (horizontally)}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| # | Derived Quantity | Equation | Use-Case in AR | Critical Threshold |
|---|------------------|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Camera FOV | $\text{FOV}=2\arctan(w/2f)$ | Lens selection | **> 40°** for usable AR |
| 2 | Latency budget | $\sum L_i < 20$ ms | Pipeline design | **< 20 ms** |
| 3 | Stereo depth | $Z = fB/d$ | Occlusion, AR placement | Real-time |
| 4 | Depth uncertainty | $\sigma_Z = (Z/d)\sigma_d$ | Tracking error analysis | **< 5% of $Z$** |
| 5 | Pixel pitch (angular) | $\theta_{px}=\text{FOV}/N_{px}$ | Display sharpness | **< 1 arc-min** |
| 6 | Frame period | $T = 1/f_{refresh}$ | Throughput | **T < 16.7 ms (60 Hz)** |
| 7 | VAC conflict | $\Delta_{\text{VAC}} = \vert d_v - d_a \vert$ | Optical comfort | **< 0.5 D** |
| 8 | Display luminance | $L \geq 2000$ nits | Outdoor AR | **≥ 2000 nits** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AR System End-to-End Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Real World Scene] --> B[Sensing Layer]
    B --> B1[IMU Accel Gyro Mag]
    B --> B2[RGB Stereo Camera]
    B --> B3[LiDAR ToF Depth]
    B --> B4[GPS WiFi BLE]
    B --> B5[Eye Tracker Mic]

    B1 --> C[Tracking and Registration]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C

    C --> C1[Marker Based ARToolKit]
    C --> C2[Markerless NFT SIFT ORB]
    C --> C3[Visual Inertial SLAM]
    C --> C4[Model Based Edge Tracking]
    C --> C5[Kalman Filter Sensor Fusion]

    C --> D[Scene Understanding]
    D --> D1[Plane Detection]
    D --> D2[Object Detection YOLO]
    D --> D3[Semantic Segmentation]
    D --> D4[Occlusion via Depth Buffer]
    D --> D5[Lighting Estimation SH]

    D --> E[Rendering and Compositing]
    E --> E1[Virtual Object Generation]
    E --> E2[Alpha Blending]
    E --> E3[Depth Compositing]
    E --> E4[Shading Consistency Pass]
    E --> E5[Frame Buffer Assembly]

    E --> F[Display Output Layer]
    F --> F1[Optical See Through OST]
    F --> F2[Video See Through VST]
    F --> F3[Spatial Projection AR]
    F --> F4[Handheld AR Phone Tablet]

    F --> G[User Perception and Interaction]
    G --> G1[Gaze Selection]
    G --> G2[Hand Gesture Tracking]
    G --> G3[Voice Command]
    G --> G4[Haptic Feedback]

    G --> H{Perceptually Seamless AR}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data flows **top → bottom**. The control loop from `User Perception` back to `Sensing Layer` (via eye/head motion) makes the AR system a **closed-loop, real-time cyber-physical system**. This is the architecture KTU expects you to draw in 14-mark ESE answers.

---

## 4.2 AR Tracking Subsystem — Sensor Fusion Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMU[IMU 200 Hz] --> KF[Extended Kalman Filter]
    CAM[Camera 30 Hz] --> FE[Feature Extractor ORB]
    FE --> POSE[Pose Estimator PnP RANSAC]
    POSE --> KF
    KF --> STATE[6 DoF Pose x y z roll pitch yaw]
    STATE --> REND[Renderer]
    IMU --> PRED[IMU Prediction Step]
    PRED --> KF
    KF --> FB[Feedback to IMU Bias Correction]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO)** architecture used in HoloLens 2, Meta Quest 3, and Magic Leap 2. The IMU runs at high rate to **predict** motion, while the camera runs at lower rate to **correct** drift — the classic **predict-update cycle** of the EKF.

---

## 4.3 Display Technology Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ARDISP[AR Display Technologies] --> OST[Optical See Through OST]
    ARDISP --> VST[Video See Through VST]

    OST --> OC[Optical Combiner]
    OST --> WG[Waveguide Holographic]
    OST --> BB[Birdbath Optics]
    OST --> PR[Prism Retro Reflective]

    VST --> CP[Camera Passthrough VR HMD]
    VST --> SS[Smartphone Screen]
    VST --> TB[Tablet Screen]
```

| Display Type | FOV | Brightness | Use-Case |
|--------------|-----|-----------|----------|
| **Waveguide (HoloLens)** | 52° | 500 nits | Enterprise AR |
| **Birdbath (VST)** | 90° | 100 nits | Consumer XR |
| **Retinal Projection** | 70° | Variable | Research |
| **Smartphone AR** | 65° | 1000 nits | Mobile AR (ARKit/ARCore) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the five functional layers of an AR system and state the role of the tracking layer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Sensing/Input Layer** — captures raw world data (IMU, camera, depth).
2. **Tracking and Registration Layer** — estimates the 6-DoF pose of the device in the world frame.
3. **Scene Understanding Layer** — detects planes, objects, and lighting conditions.
4. **Rendering/Compositing Layer** — generates virtual content and blends it with the real view.
5. **Display/Output Layer** — presents the composited frame to the user (OST or VST).

The **tracking layer** is responsible for the **6-DoF pose estimation** $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ so that virtual content is **spatially registered** to the real world in real time. *(Full marks = 3: 1 mark for listing 5 layers, 2 marks for explaining tracking.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Optical See-Through (OST) and Video See-Through (VST) AR displays. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | OST (Optical See-Through) | VST (Video See-Through) |
|--------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| Mechanism | Real world viewed **directly**; virtual content overlaid via optics | Real world **captured by camera**, then displayed with virtual content |
| Latency | Lower (no camera capture delay) | Higher (sensor + pipeline delay) |
| Safety | Higher (no display failure = see reality) | Risk if display/camera fails |
| Example | **Microsoft HoloLens 2** | **Meta Quest 3** (passthrough mode) |

*(1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for latency/safety, 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule (Module 3 Internal Choice):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Diagrams carry **2–3 marks** weight.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
#### (a) [7 Marks] — Explain in detail the end-to-end functional pipeline of an Augmented Reality system. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of each subsystem from sensor input to user display.

**Model Solution:**

The end-to-end AR pipeline converts real-world stimuli into a perceptually augmented image via five cascaded stages:

1. **Sensing / Input Stage** *(1.5 marks)*
   - **IMU** (3-axis accelerometer + gyroscope + magnetometer) → measures linear acceleration, angular velocity, heading.
   - **RGB / RGB-D / Stereo Cameras** → capture visual scene.
   - **LiDAR / ToF** → provide metric depth maps for occlusion.
   - **GPS / Wi-Fi / BLE** → outdoor and indoor localization.
   - **Eye tracker, microphone** → user intent.

2. **Tracking & Registration Stage** *(2 marks)*
   - Computes the **6-DoF pose** $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ of the camera w.r.t. the world.
   - **Algorithms:** Marker-based (fiducials), Markerless (NFT — SIFT, ORB), **SLAM** (ORB-SLAM, LSD-SLAM), Sensor-fusion via **Extended Kalman Filter**.
   - The pose is encoded as a $4\times 4$ homogeneous transformation matrix $\mathbf{T}_{world}^{cam}$.

3. **Scene Understanding Stage** *(1 mark)*
   - Detects planes (horizontal/vertical), recognises objects (YOLO, SSD), segments semantics, estimates lighting via spherical harmonics.

4. **Rendering & Compositing Stage** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Generates virtual geometry with **OpenGL ES / Vulkan**.
   - **Alpha blending** for transparent overlays, **depth compositing** for correct occlusion.
   - Applies **shading consistency** to match real lighting.

5. **Display Stage** *(1 mark)*
   - **OST** (waveguide, birdbath, prism) or **VST** (camera passthrough).
   - Final augmented frame presented to the user at **≥ 60 Hz**, **< 20 ms** latency.

**Diagram:** (Refer to **Section 4.1** Mermaid diagram — converted to neat labelled block diagram for exam sheet.) *(3 marks for labelled diagram showing all 5 layers + sensor feedback loop.)*

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — A stereo AR camera has focal length $f = 1200$ pixels, baseline $B = 70$ mm, and observes a real object with disparity $d = 5$ pixels. Compute the depth $Z$ of the object, and determine whether a 1-pixel disparity error is acceptable for an AR application that requires depth precision of **±10 cm** at this range.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Depth calculation** *(2 marks)*

$$
Z = \frac{fB}{d} = \frac{1200 \times 70}{5} = \frac{84000}{5} = 16800\;\text{mm} = 16.8\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Depth uncertainty** *(2 marks)*

$$
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} = -\frac{fB}{d^2} = -\frac{Z}{d} = -\frac{16.8}{5} = -3.36\;\text{m/pixel}
$$

$$
\sigma_Z = \vert -3.36 \vert \times 1 = 3.36\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to required precision** *(2 marks)*

Required: $\pm 0.1\;\text{m}$. Actual uncertainty: $3.36\;\text{m}$. Since $3.36 \gg 0.1$, **a 1-pixel disparity error is NOT acceptable.**

**Step 4 — Mitigation** *(1 mark)*

- Use **sub-pixel disparity estimation** (e.g., block matching with interpolation) reducing $\sigma_d$ to **0.1 pixel** → $\sigma_Z = 0.336\;\text{m}$, still insufficient.
- **Increase baseline $B$** to **$240\;\text{mm}$** → $\sigma_Z = 0.1\;\text{m}$ at the same $Z$ and $d$.

$$
\boxed{Z = 16.8\;\text{m},\;\; \sigma_Z = 3.36\;\text{m}\;\;(\text{unacceptable; widen baseline to}\geq 240\;\text{mm})}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
#### (a) [7 Marks] — Discuss the major display technologies used in AR systems. Compare Optical See-Through (OST) and Video See-Through (VST) approaches in terms of latency, image quality, safety, and field of view. State one example device for each.

**Model Solution:**

**OST Display Technologies** *(2 marks)*
- **Waveguide + Holographic Optical Elements (HOE):** Light from a micro-projector is **in-coupled**, guided by total internal reflection in a thin glass/plastic slab, and **out-coupled** to the eye. Used in **Microsoft HoloLens 2** and **Vuzix**.
- **Birdbath optics:** Two mirrors + a beam splitter fold the image path into the eye. Used in **Epson Moverio** and **Rokid Air**.
- **Prism / Freeform prism:** A simple reflective prism bends the image into the eye. Used in early **Google Glass**.

**VST Display Technologies** *(1.5 marks)*
- Camera captures the real world, GPU composites virtual content, and a **near-eye display** (LCD/OLED) shows the augmented image. Used in **Meta Quest 3 (passthrough)**, **Varjo XR-3**, **Apple Vision Pro (mostly VST)**.

**Comparison Table** *(2.5 marks)*

| Aspect | OST | VST |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Latency** | Lower (~5–10 ms) | Higher (~15–25 ms) due to camera + compositing |
| **Image Quality** | Real world at full resolution, but limited by combiner | Both real & virtual can be enhanced/relit |
| **Safety** | Higher — direct view of reality; no failure blindness | Risk of "blackout" if display/camera fails |
| **Field of View** | Limited (40°–52° current) | Can be very wide (>100°) |
| **Occlusion Handling** | Difficult (no access to real depth pixels) | Easy (full z-buffer available) |
| **Example** | HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2 | Meta Quest 3, Varjo XR-3 |

**Conclusion** *(1 mark)*: OST is preferred for **industrial / medical** (safety-critical) applications; VST is preferred for **consumer / training** applications where immersive occlusion matters.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — The motion-to-photon latency budget of an AR system is broken down as follows: sensor read 3 ms, visual-inertial tracking 4 ms, scene understanding 3 ms, GPU rendering 6 ms, display update 2 ms. (i) Compute the total latency. (ii) Determine if it satisfies the KTU acceptance criterion of **< 20 ms**. (iii) If the GPU rendering is upgraded to a 90 Hz pipeline, what is the new total and frame period? (iv) Comment on the implication of motion sickness.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total latency** *(2 marks)*

$$
L_{total} = 3 + 4 + 3 + 6 + 2 = 18\;\text{ms}
$$

**(ii) KTU compliance check** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
18\;\text{ms} < 20\;\text{ms} \;\;\checkmark \;\;\text{Compliant with}\; 2\;\text{ms headroom}
$$

**(iii) Upgrade to 90 Hz rendering pipeline** *(2 marks)*

At 90 Hz, the per-frame rendering time becomes:

$$
T_{render} = \frac{1}{90} \approx 11.11\;\text{ms}
$$

But because the system can now only **submit one frame every $11.11\;\text{ms}$**, the new total (worst-case) latency becomes:

$$
L_{total}^{\text{new}} = 3 + 4 + 3 + 11.11 + 2 = 23.11\;\text{ms}
$$

Wait — this is **counterintuitive**. The remedy is to **pipeline** the rendering so each stage runs **in parallel** with the next frame's capture. In pipelined steady state, the latency is bounded by the **slowest single stage**, not their sum. Therefore:

$$
L_{total}^{\text{pipelined}} = \max(L_{sensor}, L_{track}, L_{render}, L_{display}) = \max(3, 4, 11.11, 2) = 11.11\;\text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{total}^{\text{new}} \approx 11.11\;\text{ms} \;\;\text{with pipelining (massively under 20 ms).}}
$$

The frame period is:

$$
T_{frame} = 11.11\;\text{ms} \quad\Rightarrow\quad f = \frac{1}{T} \approx 90\;\text{Hz}
$$

**(iv) Motion-sickness implication** *(1.5 marks)*

- Latency above **20 ms** causes a mismatch between **visual motion** and **vestibular motion**, triggering **simulator sickness** (nausea, disorientation).
- Reducing latency from **18 → 11.11 ms** is **well within the safe zone** and significantly reduces cybersickness risk in long AR sessions.

$$
\boxed{\text{System is safe and KTU-compliant; 90 Hz pipelined rendering yields}\; L\approx 11.1\;\text{ms.}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in "AR Systems & Functionality"**
> 1. **Conflating AR with VR architecture** — AR *augments* reality; the **real-world input stream is mandatory**. Marks deducted if you describe a closed synthetic scene as "AR".
> 2. **Confusing 3-DoF with 6-DoF** — head tracking in AR requires **6-DoF pose** $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$, not just 3-DoF orientation.
> 3. **Forgetting the feedback loop** — The AR pipeline is **closed-loop** (user eye/head motion drives new sensor input). Drawing a one-way flow chart loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up OST and VST** — OST = *direct optical* view; VST = *camera-captured* view. Examiners test this distinction every semester.
> 5. **Skipping units in calculations** — Always state **ms, mm, m, °, nit, dioptre**. Numerical answer without units = **0.5 to 1 mark** deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting motion-to-photon threshold** — The KTU standard is **< 20 ms**. Always state this benchmark in latency questions.
> 7. **Latency in pipelined vs non-pipelined** — In a **pipelined** system, total latency ≈ **max stage time**, **not sum**. Many students lose 2 marks by summing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Last-Minute Revision — Module 3 / AR Systems & Functionality**

### **A. Core Definitions**
- AR System = real-time, 3D-registered blending of real + virtual.
- Functionality = the *complete pipeline* from sensor → display.
- 5 functional layers: **Sensing → Tracking → Scene Understanding → Rendering → Display**.

### **B. Must-Memorize Numbers (KTU 2024)**
- Motion-to-photon latency: **< 20 ms** ✅
- Refresh rate: **≥ 60 Hz** (modern AR **≥ 90 Hz**)
- Display brightness (outdoor): **≥ 2000 nits**
- Pixel pitch (retina-grade): **≤ 1 arc-minute**
- Pose accuracy: **≤ 1 mm**, **≤ 0.5°**
- Human eye FOV: **~200°**, comfortable AR FOV: **≥ 40°**

### **C. Must-Memorize Equations**
- FOV: $\text{FOV}=2\arctan(w/2f)$
- Stereo depth: $Z = fB/d$
- Depth uncertainty: $\sigma_Z = (Z/d)\sigma_d$
- Pinhole projection: $u=fX/Z$
- Frame period: $T=1/f_{refresh}$
- VAC conflict: $\Delta_{\text{VAC}} = \vert d_v - d_a \vert$

### **D. Display Technology Pairings**
- **OST** = HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2, Vuzix, Google Glass.
- **VST** = Meta Quest 3, Varjo XR-3, Apple Vision Pro.

### **E. Tracking Algorithm Family**
- Marker / Markerless / **SLAM** / Model-based.
- Sensor fusion via **EKF** (IMU + Vision = VIO).

### **F. Pipeline Key Insight**
- AR is a **closed-loop, real-time cyber-physical system**.
- The slowest stage **bottlenecks** the entire system.
- **Pipelining** = latency ≈ max stage time, not sum.

### **G. Common Mnemonics**
- **"S-T-S-R-D"** = **S**ensing, **T**racking, **S**cene, **R**endering, **D**isplay.
- **"OST sees reality, VST sees pixels"**.
- **"SLAM = Localization + Mapping, simultaneously"**.

### **H. Examiner-Favourite Two-Liners (use verbatim in exam)**
1. *"An AR system must register virtual content in the **real-world coordinate frame** with **6-DoF pose accuracy** and **< 20 ms** motion-to-photon latency."*
2. *"OST offers direct optical viewing and **lower latency**, while VST enables **easier occlusion and relighting** at the cost of safety and delay."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Augmented Reality Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Augmented Reality Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Augmented Reality (AR) Methods** are the standardized classification frameworks, algorithmic pipelines, and hardware–software interaction paradigms used to *capture*, *align*, *register*, and *blend* computer-generated virtual content with the real-world environment in real time, so that the user perceives both coexisting in the same spatial frame of reference.

According to the **Azuma (1997)** survey (the foundational definition accepted in the KTU PECPT634 syllabus):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An AR system must satisfy **three** defining properties:
> 1. **Combines real and virtual** content in a single perceptive frame.
> 2. **Interactive in real time** (latency ≤ **20 ms** for comfortable head-tracking).
> 3. **Registered in 3-D** (virtual objects must be spatially locked to real-world geometry).

**AR Methods** in Module 3 specifically refers to the **taxonomy of techniques** used to achieve these three properties — namely the *sensing*, *tracking*, and *rendering* methods.

## 1.2 The Five Canonical AR Methods (Azuma Taxonomy)

| # | Method | Core Idea | Trigger |
|---|--------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Marker-Based (Fiducial) AR** | Recognizes a known visual pattern in the scene | Pre-printed 2D marker |
| 2 | **Markerless / Natural Feature Tracking (NFT)** | Matches natural image features against a prior map | Texture / geometry of the environment |
| 3 | **Location-Based (GPS + Compass) AR** | Anchors virtual content to geo-coordinates | GNSS position + orientation |
| 4 | **Projection-Based AR** | Projects light onto physical surfaces | Spatial light modulator + depth sensor |
| 5 | **Superimposition-Based AR** | Replaces (or overlays on) a real object with a virtual one | Object recognition of a known template |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of AR Methods like different "postmen" delivering a letter (virtual content) to a house (real world):**
> - **Marker-based AR** = The postman who only delivers when he sees the *red mailbox* at your door. Easy and reliable, but the mailbox must exist.
> - **Markerless AR (NFT/SLAM)** = The postman who can read your *house number* and recognize your *door* by sight. Flexible, but takes longer to learn the route.
> - **Location-based AR** = The postman who uses *Google Maps* with lat/long coordinates. Works outdoors, useless indoors.
> - **Projection-based AR** = The postman who *paints* the message directly on your wall with a flashlight. The wall becomes the display.
> - **Superimposition-based AR** = The postman who *replaces your door* with a picture of a different door, keeping the rest of the house unchanged.

## 1.4 Key Physical / Performance Constants

- **Display refresh rate** for AR: **≥ 60 Hz** (to avoid motion sickness).
- **End-to-end latency budget** (sensor → render → display): **≤ 20 ms**.
- **Tracking accuracy threshold** for comfortable overlay: **≤ 1 pixel** at the focal plane.
- **Field of View (FoV)** of typical optical see-through HMD: **~40°–52°** (Microsoft HoloLens 2: **52°**).
- **Standard AR marker size** for reliable detection: **≥ 2 cm** at **1 m** viewing distance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Marker-Based (Fiducial) AR

A **fiducial marker** is a synthetic, high-contrast 2D pattern whose four corner positions are *exactly known a priori* (e.g., ARToolKit marker, ARTag, AprilTag).

### Operational Pipeline (8 Steps)
1. **Image acquisition** — RGB camera captures a frame $I_t$ at time $t$.
2. **Grayscale conversion** — $I_g = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$.
3. **Adaptive thresholding** — Convert to binary image $I_b(x,y) \in \{0,1\}$.
4. **Contour extraction** — Find quadrilateral 4-corner regions.
5. **Candidate verification** — Decode the inner binary pattern; check against known marker dictionary.
6. **Corner refinement** — Sub-pixel localization using the **Harris corner detector** or **GoodFeaturesToTrack**.
7. **Pose estimation** — Solve a **Perspective-n-Point (PnP)** problem using the 4 known 2D-3D correspondences to obtain rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$.
8. **OpenGL rendering** — Load $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ as the camera's *ModelView* matrix and draw the virtual object.

### Why Each Step Is Needed (The "How & Why")
- Steps 1–3 reduce data from **~1 MB** per frame to a **few KB** binary image → real-time.
- Steps 4–5 isolate and *identify* which marker is in view, not just detect something square.
- Step 6 is critical because integer-pixel corners cause **jitter** in overlay.
- Step 7 is the **mathematical heart** — converts a 2D image observation into a 3D camera pose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental matrix of marker-based AR** is the *homography* $\mathbf{H}$ — a $3 \times 3$ matrix mapping marker-plane coordinates to image-plane coordinates.

## 2.2 Method 2 — Markerless AR (NFT + SLAM)

### 2.2.1 Natural Feature Tracking (NFT)
- **Feature detection**: SIFT, SURF, ORB, BRISK → keypoint set $\mathcal{K} = \{k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n\}$.
- **Descriptor matching**: Compare descriptors against a *prior feature map* $\mathcal{M}$ of the target object/scene.
- **Homography estimation** from $\geq 4$ matches using **RANSAC** to reject outliers.

### 2.2.2 SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)
- Builds a sparse 3D map *while simultaneously* estimating the camera pose.
- Front-end: feature tracking (KLT optical flow on FAST corners).
- Back-end: **Bundle Adjustment** minimizing the reprojection error:
$$E_{BA} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{S}(i)} \rho\Big( \big\| \pi(\mathbf{R}_i \mathbf{X}_j + \mathbf{t}_i) - \mathbf{x}_{ij} \big\|^2 \Big)$$
where $\rho(\cdot)$ is a robust Huber loss, $\pi$ is the camera projection, $\mathbf{X}_j$ is a 3D landmark, and $\mathbf{x}_{ij}$ is its 2D observation.

## 2.3 Method 3 — Location-Based AR
- Uses **GPS** (or BeiDou / GLONASS) for $\phi, \lambda, h$ (latitude, longitude, altitude).
- Uses **magnetometer + accelerometer + gyroscope** (9-DOF IMU) for orientation $(\psi, \theta, \phi)$ (yaw, pitch, roll).
- Geodetic-to-ECEF conversion:
$$X = (N + h)\cos\phi\cos\lambda$$
$$Y = (N + h)\cos\phi\sin\lambda$$
$$Z = \big( N(1-e^2) + h \big)\sin\phi$$
where $N = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{1 - e^2 \sin^2 \phi}}$ is the radius of curvature, $a$ is the **WGS-84 semi-major axis** = **6378137.0 m**, and $e^2$ is the **WGS-84 eccentricity squared** ≈ **0.00669437999**.

## 2.4 Method 4 — Projection-Based AR
- A **digital light projector (DLP)** or **laser** paints structured light onto a real surface.
- A **depth camera** (e.g., Microsoft Kinect, structured-light sensor) reconstructs the surface mesh.
- Virtual content is rendered *into* the projection mapping coordinates.

> [!TIP]
> **Spatial Augmented Reality (SAR)** is a sub-class of projection AR where the projected image *adapts to* the geometry of the physical surface (e.g., projecting a keyboard onto a desk).

## 2.5 Method 5 — Superimposition-Based AR
- The real object is **fully replaced** by a virtual one (or a partially overlaid augmentation).
- Requires **object recognition** (template matching, CNN classifiers like YOLO, MobileNet).
- Pose of the real object → pose of the virtual object.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| Grayscale conversion | $I_g = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | **ITU-R BT.601 luma weights** |
| Homography | $\mathbf{x}' \sim \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$ | $3\times 3$ projective transform, **8 DOF** |
| Reprojection error | $e_i = \lVert \pi(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{X}_i + \mathbf{t}) - \mathbf{x}_i \rVert$ | in **pixels** |
| Camera intrinsics (pinhole) | $x = f\dfrac{X}{Z},\; y = f\dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | focal length $f$ in **pixels** |
| Geodetic → ECEF | see Section 2.3 above | WGS-84 datum |
| SLAM bundle adjustment | see Section 2.2.2 | **non-linear least squares** |
| End-to-end latency budget | $t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{track} + t_{render} + t_{display}$ | must be $\leq$ **20 ms** |
| SLAM error propagation | $\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_k \mathbf{F}_k^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}_k$ | **EKF covariance update** |
| Optical flow constraint | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | **Horn–Schunck / Lucas–Kanade** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on absolute value notation in tables:** In the formulas above, the symbol $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ is used (rather than the vertical pipe `\vert` character) to prevent breaking markdown table parsing.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Marker-based AR**: surgical instrument tracking, factory floor KPI dashboards, KTU lab demos.
- **Markerless (SLAM) AR**: Pokémon GO, IKEA Place, HoloLens 2 industrial maintenance.
- **Location-based AR**: Google Maps Live View, tourism apps (e.g., ViewRanger).
- **Projection-based AR**: automotive head-up displays (HUDs), interactive exhibitions.
- **Superimposition AR**: virtual try-on (Ray-Ban × Meta, Lenskart AR), medical anatomy visualization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Homography Matrix for a Planar Marker

This is the single most important derivation for KTU's AR Methods module. A planar marker lies on the plane $Z = 0$ in its own local coordinate frame. We want to find the $3\times3$ matrix $\mathbf{H}$ that maps a point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, 0)^{\top}$ in the marker frame to its image-plane observation $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, 1)^{\top}$ (in homogeneous form).

### Step 1 — Camera Projection Equation

A 3D point $\mathbf{X}_w$ in world coordinates projects to a 2D image point $\mathbf{x}$ as:

$$\mathbf{x} \sim \mathbf{K}\left[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}\right]\mathbf{X}_w$$

where $\mathbf{K}$ is the $3 \times 3$ camera intrinsic matrix, $\mathbf{R}$ is the $3 \times 3$ rotation, and $\mathbf{t}$ is the $3 \times 1$ translation.

### Step 2 — Specialize to the Marker Plane

Since the marker lies at $Z = 0$, we write $\mathbf{X}_w = (X, Y, 0, 1)^{\top}$ (homogeneous). The transformation becomes:

$$\mathbf{K}\left[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}\right]\begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{K}\left(\mathbf{r}_1 X + \mathbf{r}_2 Y + \mathbf{t}\right)$$

where $\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2$ are the first two columns of $\mathbf{R}$. The third column $\mathbf{r}_3$ vanishes because it multiplies $Z=0$.

### Step 3 — Define the Homography

Collecting the two planar degrees of freedom into a single $3 \times 3$ matrix:

$$\mathbf{H} \triangleq \mathbf{K}\left[\mathbf{r}_1 \mid \mathbf{r}_2 \mid \mathbf{t}\right]$$

This is the **homography matrix**. The mapping simplifies to:

$$\mathbf{x} \sim \mathbf{H}\begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Decomposition (Recovering Pose from $\mathbf{H}$)

Given a calibrated camera ($\mathbf{K}$ known), we recover pose as follows. Write $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}\left[\mathbf{r}_1 \mid \mathbf{r}_2 \mid \mathbf{t}\right]$ and separate:

$$\mathbf{h}_1 = \lambda \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{H}_{(1)} \quad (\text{first column})$$
$$\mathbf{h}_2 = \lambda \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{H}_{(2)} \quad (\text{second column})$$
$$\mathbf{h}_3 = \lambda \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{H}_{(3)} \quad (\text{third column})$$

where $\lambda$ is a scalar. Since $\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2$ are orthonormal ($\mathbf{r}_1^{\top}\mathbf{r}_1 = \mathbf{r}_2^{\top}\mathbf{r}_2 = 1$ and $\mathbf{r}_1^{\top}\mathbf{r}_2 = 0$):

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\lVert \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{H}_{(1)} \rVert} = \dfrac{1}{\lVert \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{H}_{(2)} \rVert}$$

The recovered rotation columns are:

$$\mathbf{r}_1 = \lambda \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{H}_{(1)}, \quad \mathbf{r}_2 = \lambda \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{H}_{(2)}$$

$$\mathbf{r}_3 = \mathbf{r}_1 \times \mathbf{r}_2, \quad \mathbf{t} = \lambda \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{H}_{(3)}$$

### Step 5 — Enforce Orthonormality (Procrustes Correction)

Because of image noise, the recovered $\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2$ may not be exactly orthogonal. We run a **SVD-based correction**:

$$\mathbf{R}_{refined} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{V}^{\top} \quad \text{where } \mathbf{U}\Sigma \mathbf{V}^{\top} = \text{svd}\left(\left[\mathbf{r}_1 \mid \mathbf{r}_2 \mid \mathbf{r}_1 \times \mathbf{r}_2\right]\right)$$

This guarantees a valid rotation matrix with $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$.

## 3.2 Derivation: End-to-End Latency Budget

The total latency experienced by a user from real-world motion to seeing the corresponding virtual update is:

$$t_{total} = t_{sensor} + t_{pose} + t_{render} + t_{display}$$

Typical component values for a modern optical see-through HMD (e.g., HoloLens 2-class device):

$$t_{sensor} \approx 4 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(global shutter CMOS)}$$

$$t_{pose} \approx 3 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(SLAM + IMU fusion)}$$

$$t_{render} \approx 6 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(single-pass stereo)}$$

$$t_{display} \approx 2 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(LCD response time)}$$

Summing:

$$t_{total} \approx 4 + 3 + 6 + 2 = 15 \text{ ms} \leq 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of AR Methods

| Property | Marker-Based | Markerless (NFT/SLAM) | Location-Based | Projection-Based | Superimposition |
|----------|:------------:|:---------------------:|:--------------:|:----------------:|:---------------:|
| **Setup cost** | Low (print marker) | Medium (build feature map) | None (GPS) | High (projector) | Medium (3D model) |
| **Indoor use** | Excellent | Excellent | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| **Outdoor use** | Limited | Good | Excellent | Limited | Good |
| **Tracking accuracy** | High (±1 mm at 0.5 m) | Medium (±5 mm) | Low (±5 m) | High (surface-aligned) | Object-dependent |
| **Computational cost** | Very low | High (SLAM) | Very low | Medium | Medium |
| **Failure mode** | Occlusion of marker | Featureless environment | GPS-denied area | Ambient light interference | Misrecognition |
| **Typical use case** | KTU lab, assembly line | HoloLens, mobile AR | Tourism, navigation | Car HUD, art installation | Try-on apps |
| **Latency** | < 5 ms | 10–20 ms | 50–200 ms (GPS) | 16 ms (1 frame) | 10–20 ms |

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Frequently Asked in KTU)

**Q:** A marker is placed parallel to the camera image plane at distance $d = 500$ mm. The focal length is $f = 800$ pixels. What is the size of a feature in the image if it is $20$ mm tall on the marker?

**Solution:**

$$h_{image} = f \cdot \frac{h_{world}}{d} = 800 \cdot \frac{20}{500}$$

$$h_{image} = \frac{16000}{500} = 32 \text{ pixels}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows the **inverse-proportionality** of image feature size with distance — a foundational relationship in marker-based AR sizing.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]`
**Differentiate between marker-based and markerless Augmented Reality. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Marker-Based AR | Markerless AR |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| **Trigger** | Requires a known 2D fiducial pattern (e.g., ARTag) | Works on natural features or 3D geometry |
| **Setup** | Marker must be printed and visible | Requires prior 3D map / SLAM initialization |
| **Computational load** | Very low — binary pattern matching | High — feature detection + SLAM |
| **Indoor accuracy** | ± 1 mm (excellent) | ± 5 mm (good) |

**Advantages (1 mark for any one valid point per method):**
- **Marker-based**: (i) Highly reliable detection, (ii) Low computational cost suitable for mobile.
- **Markerless**: (i) No need to modify the environment, (ii) Works on arbitrary real-world surfaces.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]`
**What is SLAM? Explain its role in markerless AR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** SLAM (**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping**) is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's (camera's) location within that map.

**Role in Markerless AR (2 marks):**
1. **Localization** — Provides continuous **6-DOF camera pose** (position + orientation) without needing any fiducial marker.
2. **Mapping** — Builds a sparse 3D point cloud of the environment in real time, which serves as the *reference frame* for placing virtual content.
3. **Loop closure** — Detects re-visited places to **drift-correct** the pose estimate, enabling long-duration AR sessions with bounded error.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **marker-based AR pipeline** in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe each stage from image acquisition to virtual content rendering.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **homography matrix** $\mathbf{H}$ for a planar marker and show how the **camera pose** $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ is recovered from it.

---

**Solution:**

#### (a) Marker-Based AR Pipeline (7 marks)

The pipeline has **8 stages** as shown in the diagram from Section 4.2. Each stage carries partial marks as follows:

**Stage 1 — Image Acquisition (0.5 mark):**
A monocular RGB camera (typically **30–60 fps**) captures a frame $I_t$ at each time step. The resolution is usually **640×480** to **1920×1080**.

**Stage 2 — Grayscale Conversion (0.5 mark):**
Using the ITU-R BT.601 luma formula:
$$I_g(x,y) = 0.299 I_R(x,y) + 0.587 I_G(x,y) + 0.114 I_B(x,y)$$
This reduces the data volume by ~3× and removes color dependence.

**Stage 3 — Adaptive Thresholding (0.5 mark):**
The grayscale image is binarized using a local (adaptive) threshold:
$$I_b(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } I_g(x,y) > \tau(x,y) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
where $\tau(x,y)$ is a local mean of the $N \times N$ neighbourhood. This handles uneven illumination.

**Stage 4 — Contour Extraction (0.5 mark):**
Connected component analysis finds **black quadrilateral borders** in $I_b$. The Suzuki–Topological algorithm or OpenCV's `findContours()` is used.

**Stage 5 — Marker Identification (1 mark):**
The inner bit pattern of each candidate quadrilateral is decoded by warping the quadrilateral to a canonical square and reading a binary code. This is matched against a known dictionary (e.g., **ARToolKit: 1024 markers**, **ARTag: 2002 markers**).

**Stage 6 — Sub-pixel Corner Refinement (1 mark):**
Corners are localized to sub-pixel accuracy using either:
- **Harris corner detector**: $R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k(\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}))^2$ with $k \approx 0.04$.
- **Good Features to Track** (Shi–Tomasi): $\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2) > T$.

**Stage 7 — Pose Estimation (1.5 marks):**
The Perspective-n-Point problem is solved for $n = 4$ correspondences. Using the Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm, we minimize:
$$\min_{\mathbf{R},\mathbf{t}} \sum_{i=1}^{4} \big\| \pi(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{X}_i + \mathbf{t}) - \mathbf{x}_i \big\|^2$$

**Stage 8 — OpenGL Rendering (1 mark):**
The estimated $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ becomes the **ModelView matrix** in OpenGL. The virtual object is drawn as a 3D mesh, projected through the same camera intrinsics, and composited on top of the live video frame in a shader.

**Block diagram (0.5 mark):** Reproduce the pipeline of Section 4.2.

---

#### (b) Homography Derivation (7 marks)

This is a direct test of the derivation in Section 3.1. The student must produce:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the camera projection equation $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}|\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}_w$.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Specialize to the marker plane $Z=0$:
$$\mathbf{K}\left[\mathbf{R}\mid\mathbf{t}\right]\begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{K}\left(\mathbf{r}_1 X + \mathbf{r}_2 Y + \mathbf{t}\right)$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Define $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}\left[\mathbf{r}_1 \mid \mathbf{r}_2 \mid \mathbf{t}\right]$ and write $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathbf{H}\bar{\mathbf{X}}$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Pose recovery — extract $\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \mathbf{t}$ from $\mathbf{H}$ using $\lambda = 1/\lVert\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{H}_{(1)}\rVert$, then enforce orthonormality via SVD.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State the **3×3 structure of $\mathbf{H}$** and its **8 degrees of freedom** (9 entries up to scale).

**Numerical sub-question (0.5 mark bonus):** Solve $h_{image} = 32$ pixels example.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **five major AR methods** in a comparison table. For each method, give one **specific real-world application** and one **failure mode**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **SLAM-based AR** approach. With a diagram, describe the **front-end and back-end** of a Visual-Inertial SLAM system and explain the role of **bundle adjustment**.

---

**Solution Sketch:**

#### (a) AR Methods Comparison (7 marks)

Use the **Analytical Comparison Matrix** from Section 3.3. Marks distribution:

- Five methods correctly identified and characterized — **3 marks** (0.6 per method).
- Five correct real-world applications (one per method) — **2 marks** (0.4 each).
- Five correct failure modes (one per method) — **2 marks** (0.4 each).

**Expected application set (sample answer):**

| Method | Application | Failure Mode |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Marker-based | Surgical tool tracking in operating rooms | Marker occlusion |
| Markerless (SLAM) | Microsoft HoloLens industrial maintenance | Featureless / textureless walls |
| Location-based | Pokémon GO, Google Maps Live View | Indoors / GPS-denied zones |
| Projection-based | BMW head-up display, museum exhibits | Direct sunlight on the surface |
| Superimposition | Lenskart virtual try-on | Wrong object recognition |

---

#### (b) SLAM-Based AR (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** SLAM jointly estimates camera pose and a 3D map of the environment in real time.

**Front-End (2.5 marks):**
- **Feature detection** — ORB, FAST, or LSD-SLAM line features.
- **Tracking** — KLT optical flow, $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$.
- **Frame-to-frame visual odometry** via essential matrix $\mathbf{E}$ decomposition.

**Back-End (2.5 marks):**
- **Keyframe selection** — Insert new keyframe when translation > threshold.
- **Local bundle adjustment** — Refine last N keyframes and visible landmarks.
- **Loop closure** — Bag-of-words place recognition (DBoW2).
- **Pose graph optimization** — g²o, gtsam.

**Bundle Adjustment Role (1 mark):**
Minimizes total reprojection error:
$$\min_{\{\mathbf{R}_i,\mathbf{t}_i,\mathbf{X}_j\}} \sum_{i,j} \big\| \pi(\mathbf{R}_i\mathbf{X}_j + \mathbf{t}_i) - \mathbf{x}_{ij} \big\|^2$$
Solved by **Levenberg–Marquardt** or **Ceres Solver** with sparse Schur complement trick for efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "tracking" with "registration."** Tracking = estimating pose. Registration = aligning virtual content to that pose. Both are required.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the 3 properties of AR** (Azuma's definition). Examiners award 1 full mark just for stating them.
> 3. **Skipping the homography derivation steps.** A 7-mark question on marker-based AR *always* asks for the homography — at least sketch $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathbf{H}\mathbf{X}$ and explain $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{r}_1|\mathbf{r}_2|\mathbf{t}]$.
> 4. **Stating "SLAM uses GPS"** — incorrect. Visual SLAM is *vision-only*; GPS-SLAM is a hybrid. Don't confuse.
> 5. **Ignoring units in numerical problems.** "20 mm" not "20"; "$f$ in pixels" not just "$f$".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this and revise the night before the exam.**

- ☐ **Azuma's 3 AR properties** — combines real + virtual, real-time, registered in 3D.
- ☐ **Five canonical AR methods** — Marker, Markerless (NFT/SLAM), Location, Projection, Superimposition.
- ☐ **Marker-based pipeline** — 8 stages from grayscale to OpenGL render.
- ☐ **Homography** — $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{r}_1|\mathbf{r}_2|\mathbf{t}]$, $3\times 3$, **8 DOF**.
- ☐ **Pose recovery** — orthonormality + SVD Procrustes correction.
- ☐ **SLAM = Localization + Mapping simultaneously**.
- ☐ **Bundle Adjustment** — minimizes reprojection error via LM.
- ☐ **Loop closure** — corrects accumulated drift.
- ☐ **Location-based AR** uses **WGS-84 datum** (semi-major axis **6378137 m**).
- ☐ **Projection AR** → Spatial AR (SAR) on real surfaces.
- ☐ **Superimposition AR** → virtual try-on, full object replacement.
- ☐ **End-to-end latency budget** ≤ **20 ms**.
- ☐ **Display refresh** ≥ **60 Hz** to avoid simulator sickness.
- ☐ **Sub-pixel corner refinement** is essential for jitter-free overlay.
- ☐ **Grayscale formula** — $0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ (ITU-R BT.601).
- ☐ **Optical flow constraint** — $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$.
- ☐ **EKF covariance update** — $\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_k \mathbf{F}_k^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}_k$.
- ☐ **Common failure modes** — marker occlusion, featureless scene, GPS-denied indoor, sunlight, misrecognition.

---
